segments.s266-g41: نشستنگه (compound noun) precedes the verb as the direct object/phrase being established; خسروی is an adjectival/attributive form 'royal' modifying نشستنگه; ساختند is the past tense plural verb 'they made/built.' Word order here is noun + modifier + verb (object before verb), typical of poetic Persian where the action (ساختند) closes the clause.
segments.s266-g42: فراوان modifies تگاور and functions as a quantifier/adjective 'many/ample.' تگاور is the plural group ('riders/warriors'), برون is the adverb 'out/forth,' and تاختند is the past plural 'they rode/rushed' (intransitive motion/charge). The clause is a simple nominal phrase + adverb + verb describing the mass departure.
segments.s266-g43: بفرمود is a courtly/past form meaning 'he commanded/said' (honorific usage). تا here is the subordinating particle 'so that / in order that' introducing the subordinate clause. رفت is the past singular 'went,' and پیشش is the prepositional phrase 'before him / in his presence' (پیش + enclitic -ش). هجیر is the proper name of the person sent ahead.
segments.s266-g44: جوانی is an appositive/noun 'a youth / a young man' describing هجیر. به کردار is a manner phrase: به + کردار = 'in the manner/appearance,' where به functions adverbially ('in/as'). هشیار و پیر are two adjectives linked by و: 'shrewd/alert and old/aged,' describing the young man's appearance or bearing (a poetic contrast).
segments.s266-g45: بگفت is the past tense 'he said/uttered.' آن سخن 'that speech' is the direct object. سر به سر is an idiomatic repeated noun phrase here meaning 'entirely / word for word' (the duplication is emphatic/poetic), and پهلوان identifies the speaker or addressee as 'the champion/hero.' The verb phrase covers the act of speaking the whole speech.
segments.s266-g46: به پیش = 'to / before / in the presence of.' هشیوار modifies پور (son) and پور جوان is a nominal group 'the alert/shrewd son, the young man' (پور as 'son/descendant' used as identifier). The whole phrase marks the addressee of the speech: it was said in front of / to the alert young son.
segments.s266-g47: بدو is the enclitic prepositional phrase 'to him / unto him' (be + 3rd‑person clitic). گفت here is the past 'he said' introducing direct speech; کای is the vocative marker 'O' addressing the addressee; پور هشیاردل is a compound address 'son of the vigilant‑hearted' (پور = 'son/descendant', هشیاردل = 'alert/sensible‑hearted'), functioning as the vocative name.
segments.s266-g48: یکی = 'one / a certain' (here 'one [unit]'). تیز گردان is a noun phrase meaning 'a swift troop/detachment' (تیز = 'swift', گردان = 'troop/unit'). بدین is the fused prepositional phrase 'to/for this' (here shown as ب + دین in the segmentation); کار = 'task/affair' and دل is used poetically 'of the heart' or 'of concern' (a genitive/qualifying noun), so بدین کار دل = 'for this task/affair.' The whole clause is an imperative/sentential command content quoted after گفت.
segments.s266-g57: This clause has a poetic preposition z (ز) meaning 'from' followed by the noun 'لشکر' (host/army). The direct object is 'دو تن را' ('two persons' marked by object particle را). 'بر خویش خواند' is a verbal phrase where بر + خویش (literally 'to himself') marks the beneficiary/goal and خواند is the past tense 'called/summoned' (here in the sense 'to call/summon'). Word order is: prepositional source → object (with را) → goal phrase → verb.
segments.s266-g58: This clause continues with the result/action: 'سبکشان' ('their light ones' — here referring to light-armed men or light troops) is the thing being placed; 'به اسب تگاور' is a prepositional phrase 'onto/onto (swift) steeds' where به marks direction/place; تگاور qualifies/associates with اسب (a martial/horseman-related word). نشاند is an archaic/transitive past tense meaning 'set/placed' (here 'set upon/seated on'). The verb takes as object the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s266-g59: 'برون شد' is a two-word verbal expression meaning 'went out' or 'came forth' (برون + شد). It is followed by the source phrase 'ز پرده‌سرای پدر' where ز marks 'from' and پرده‌سرای ('curtained chamber/stage/tent-room') is the place; پدر is the genitival modifier 'father's'. The verb is simple past and the whole phrase indicates departure from a location.
segments.s266-g60: 'به هر منزلی' is a circumstantial prepositional phrase 'at/to each stopping-place' (به introducing location/time, هر 'each'). 'بر هیونی دگر' means 'upon another beast' where بر = 'on/upon' and دگر = 'other/another'; هیونی is the noun 'beast/animal'. Together this clause describes that at every halt he mounted a different animal.
segments.s266-g61: This noun-list subject 'خور و خواب و آرامشان' ('food/meal, sleep, and their rest') is followed by the locative phrase 'بر ستور' ('upon steeds/horses') where بر = 'on/upon' and ستور = 'steed/horse'. The predicate is implied ('were' / located on); in classical Persian such existence/location can be rendered without a copula. آرامشان uses the possessive suffix to mean 'their rest.'
segments.s266-g62: The pair of parallel exclamatory clauses 'چه تاریکی شب چه تابنده هور' uses چه … چه … to mean 'whether … or …'. تاریکی شب = 'the darkness of night'; تابنده هور = 'the shining light' (هور = radiance/light). The structure contrasts conditions under which the previous clause (their food/sleep being on steeds) holds.
segments.s266-g63: 'بر آن گونه' is an adverbial phrase 'in that manner' (بر + demonstrative آن + گونه 'way/manner'). 'پویان' is a participle/adjective meaning 'moving/active' modifying the group, and 'به راه آمدند' is a fixed verbal phrase 'set out on the road' (به = onto/into, راه = road, آمدند = they came/they went). Word order places the manner phrase before the verb phrase, typical in Persian narrative.
segments.s266-g64: 'به یک هفته نزدیک شاه آمدند' is a temporal/locational clause: 'به یک هفته نزدیک' means 'near/about one week' (i.e., within or after about a week) where نزدیک functions adverbially 'nearly/about', and 'شاه آمدند' literally 'they came to the king' (آمدند = they came; شاه is the goal 'the king'). Overall it means they approached or reached the king after roughly a week.
segments.s266-g65: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. The phrase uses postpositional از + راه + ایران to mean 'from the road of Iran', and بیامد is a simple past verb 'came'; سوار (rider/horseman) is the subject that precedes the verb, a common flexible word order in classical Persian.
segments.s266-g66: This clause is a simple past statement: آمد 'arrived/came'. بر followed by a person (خسرو) here means 'to/toward' or 'before' (i.e. came into the presence of). نامدار is an adjective 'renowned' modifying خسرو, which functions as definite object/topic marked implicitly by word order.
segments.s266-g67: پذیره here denotes 'a reception; those who receive' (a party or reception). فرستاد is past 'sent' (he sent). شماخ را, with the object marker را, names the recipient/target (Shamak) of the sending — i.e. 'sent (people) to receive Shamak'.
segments.s266-g68: This is an exclamatory / interrogative nominal phrase: چه مایه = 'what sort/what kind (of)'. دلیران گستاخ (plural) is the noun phrase 'bold/valiant, insolent ones'. The final را is a topical/object marker attaching the whole phrase as the content of the inquiry/exclamation.
segments.s266-g69: بپرسید is a past-tense reporting verb 'they asked' (classical past form). چون here introduces a temporal/subordinate clause 'when'/'as' (classical usage). دید is the past 'saw' and روی (face) is the direct object; هجیر is the personal name of whose face was seen.
segments.s266-g70: که‌ای is a vocative/interrogative sequence ('who are you? / O who ...') introducing direct speech. پهلوان‌زاده is a compound epithet 'champion-born' (son of a hero) and شیرگیر is an epithet 'lion-catcher' (a heroic epithet); the three words together form the address 'Who are you, O champion-born, lion-catcher?'.
segments.s266-g71: درودست باری is a literary/poetic greeting phrase (درودست = greeting/gesture of salute; باری here functions as an interjection 'indeed' or 'for' introducing the reason). که introduces the following clause 'for/since', بس is an intensifier 'very/so', and ناگهان means 'suddenly'.
segments.s266-g72: رسیدی is second-person past 'you arrived/you have come'. The preposition به + نزدیک gives 'to/into the vicinity of' and شاه جهان is a standard royal title 'the king of the world' (شاه modified by جهان). The clause is direct speech reporting the greeting's content.
segments.s266-g81: This is a prepositional/nominal string: ز + proper name + وز + epithets. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; گودرز is the proper name Goudarz. وز here is the fused poetic sequence و + ز meaning 'and from' (common in classical verse), and مهتران سپاه = 'the chiefs of the army' (مهتران = chiefs, سپاه = army). Word order is juxtapositional rather than using an explicit izāfa.
segments.s266-g82: ز هر یک یکایک is an object/source phrase: 'from each one, one by one.' یکایک ('one‑by‑one') intensifies هر ('each'). بپرسید is the past/preterite literary form 'asked' (ب‑ prefix common in classical Persian pasts) and شاه at the end is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s266-g83: درود بزرگان is a noun phrase 'the salutations/greetings of the great ones (the nobles)'; به خسرو marks the indirect object 'to Khosrow'. بداد here is the verb 'gave/ bestowed' in literary past form (verb at sentence end), connecting back to the implied agent in the preceding line.
segments.s266-g84: همه کار لشکر is a genitive/possessive phrase 'all the affairs/works of the army' (لشکر modifying کار). بر او کرد یاد is a periphrastic causative/phrasing: کرد (did) + یاد (remembrance/mention) with بر او ('upon him/to him') yields 'he caused remembrance to come upon him' → 'he reminded him' or 'he brought to his mind'.
segments.s266-g85: بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him'; داد is the past 'gave' (here the verb). پس is an adverb 'then' linking the action to what follows; نامه پهلوان is the direct object 'the champion's letter' (noun + noun in apposition/possessive relation).
segments.s266-g86: This is a string of epithets modifying پهلوان in the previous segment: جوان (young), خردمند (wise), روشن‑روان (lit. 'bright‑souled' — an epithet). In classical Persian adjectives and epithets often follow the noun they qualify.
segments.s266-g87: نویسنده را is the definite object 'the scribe' marked by the object marker را. پیش means 'in the presence of / before (him/the king)'. بنشاندند is the past plural 'they seated' (literary b‑ prefix with past), so 'they seated the scribe before him.'
segments.s266-g88: بفرمود is an honorific past 'he commanded/said' (polite form). تا introduces purpose/result 'so that' and governs the following clause; نامه is the object 'the letter'; برخواندند is the verb 'read aloud' (classical past/plural form used here in the subordinate clause), giving an order: 'he commanded that they read the letter aloud.'
segments.s266-g89: 'چو' is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. 'برخواند' is an archaic/poetic past-tense verb (3rd person singular) meaning 'read aloud' or 'recited'; the subject is omitted (understood from context). 'نامه' is the direct object and 'به' introduces the indirect object 'خسرو دبیر' (to Khosrow, the scribe). Word order here is slightly marked/poetic but follows Persian V‑O with the indirect object introduced by 'به'.
segments.s266-g90: This is a compact adjectival phrase describing 'دهان هجیر'. 'ز' is the prepositional particle 'from/of' used poetically to mark material/quality ('of ruby'). 'یاقوت رخشان' = 'ruby‑bright' (quality), and together 'ز یاقوت رخشان' modifies 'دهان هجیر' (Hojir's mouth). The prepositional phrase is fronted for poetic effect; syntactically it attaches to the following noun phrase.
segments.s266-g91: 'بیاگند' here functions as a past/performative verb meaning roughly 'they came/they went and said' (a poetic/archaic verb form); the supplied lexical senses did not include the speech sense, so this is treated as an irregular/poetic usage. 'وز آن پس' = 'and after that/then' (a temporal connective). 'به گنجور گفت' = 'said to the ganjur/treasury' where 'به' marks the indirect object ('to the treasury/treasurer') and 'گفت' is the main verb 'said' (past). Word order is typical Persian (verb-final in the clause), but the line is compacted for verse.
segments.s266-g92: 'که' is the complementizer introducing reported speech ('that' / 'saying that...' or equivalently here the content of what was said). 'دینار و دیبا' are the coordinated direct objects ('dinar(s) and brocade'). 'بیار' is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'bring'; it governs the objects. 'از نهفت' is a prepositional phrase 'from (the) hidden [place]' where 'نهفت' functions as a noun/adjective 'hidden, concealed' specifying source. The clause is an imperative reported by the preceding 'گفت'.
segments.s266-g93: 'بیاورد' is a past-tense verb 'brought' (3rd person). 'بدره' is a poetic epithet meaning 'the full‑moon (i.e., a moon‑like beauty)' and is the direct object of 'بیاورد'. 'چو' introduces the temporal subordinate clause 'when' and 'فرمان شنید' = 'heard the command' (شنید past 'heard'). The temporal clause 'چو فرمان شنید' explains when the bringing occurred.
segments.s266-g94: 'همی' is a progressive/adverbial particle marking continued or habitual action ('kept on / continuously'). 'ریخت' is a past form of ریختن here meaning 'fell/poured' (an intransitive continuous action). 'تا' is the conjunction 'until' introducing a result clause 'شد سرش ناپدید' = 'his head became vanished'. Note 'ش' is the enclitic possessive 'his/its' attached to 'سر'. The sequence expresses that something kept falling until his head disappeared.
segments.s266-g95: 'بیاورد' is the past-tense verb 'brought' (3rd person). 'پس' is a temporal/consecutive adverb 'then/after that'. 'جامه زرنگار' is the noun phrase 'a garment/robe (جامه) adorned with gold (زرنگار)'; in Persian the modifier follows the noun in poetry and 'زرنگار' functions as an adjective or adjectival noun here.
segments.s266-g96: 'چنان چون' is a fixed poetic formula meaning 'such as / just as'. 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. 'از در شهریار' is a prepositional phrase 'from the king's gate/house/court' where 'در' denotes the king's gate/entrance or courtly presence. Together the phrase qualifies the robe as the sort one would see 'from the king's court'—a marker of royal quality; word order is a poetic inversion but the structure is copula + locative/source phrase.
segments.s266-g113: از او خواست is a simple past clause meaning 'asked of him' — از marks the source/recipient ('from/of him'), او is the third‑person pronoun, and خواست is the past of خواستن used here as 'requested/asked'. پیروزی و فرهی form the direct‑object noun phrase (two nouns joined by و); فرهی is an archaic/poetic noun meaning 'nobility/glory' placed after پیروزی in a coordinate list.
segments.s266-g114: بدو جست is another verbal clause: بدو ('to him/unto him') is the indirect/object pronoun and جست is the past of جستن used in the sense 'sought' (poetic past). دیهیم و تخت مهی are the objects sought; note the noun + adjective order in تخت مهی (takh­t + mahī) where مهی is an adjective 'moon‑like/bright' following the noun.
segments.s266-g115: به یزدان بنالید is 'he bewailed/lamented to God': به marks the indirect object ('to God'), یزدان is 'God', and بنالید is the past of نالیدن meaning 'lamented'. ز افراسیاب uses ز in the causal sense 'on account of/about' followed by the proper name افراسیاب.
segments.s266-g116: به درد is an adverbial phrase 'with pain/in anguish' (به + درد). از دو دیده gives the source 'from his two eyes' (از + number + دیده). فرو ریخت is a compound where فرو (down) modifies ریخت (past of ریختن, 'poured/fell'), and آب is the object — idiomatically 'water poured from his eyes' = he wept.
segments.s266-g117: وز آنجا بیامد is a past clause 'from there he came'; وز here functions as an archaic/formal prepositional form meaning 'from' (parallel to از), آنجا is 'there', and بیامد is the past of آمدن 'came'. چو سرو سهی is a simile: چو = 'like/as', سرو = 'cypress' and سهی is a poetic epithet meaning 'dark/tall' following the noun.
segments.s266-g118: نشست is the past tense 'he sat'. از بر گاه is a prepositional phrase indicating position/at‑the‑presence ('at the court') where از بر + گاه together mark location in classical usage, and شاههنشهی is an adjective 'imperial/royal' modifying گاه ('the royal/imperial court').
segments.s266-g17: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/about' introducing what is being remembered. هومان و نستیهن are two proper names joined by و ('and'), and جنگجوی (an adjective/noun 'warrior') follows them as an epithet; Persian can place such a descriptive noun after the names it qualifies.
segments.s266-g18: سراسر همه is an emphatic adverbial phrase 'entirely/wholly' modifying یاد کرد; یاد کرد is the past tense compound 'made remembrance / remembered' (کرد is the past verb). اندر اوی literally 'in him' (اوی = 'him/it') indicates the memory was held within him; اوی is an archaic/poetic third‑person form.
segments.s266-g19: ز introduces the topic 'concerning' (کردار بیژن = 'Bijan's deed' with the possessor following the noun). که here links the noun phrase to the time/clause 'روز نبرد' ('the day of battle') — a relative/temporal connection common in classical Persian.
segments.s266-g20: بدان is effectively 'به' + demonstrative/pronominal element meaning 'to him/unto that' (here referring back to Bijan). گرزداران توران is the agent 'the mace‑bearers of Turan' (گرزداران a plural noun), and چه کرد is the interrogative 'what did (they) do?' — کرد is classical simple past used here in an epic/past narrative.
segments.s266-g21: سخن 'the tale' is the topic; سر به سر is an idiomatic adverbial 'from beginning to end / completely.' چون همه گفته بود = 'as everyone had said' — چون = 'as', همه = 'everyone/all', and گفته بود is the past perfect construction ('had said') with بود as auxiliary.
segments.s266-g22: ز پیکار و جنگ 'about/from the fight and war' uses ز to mark source/topic. آن 'that' points to the specific contest, and کجا رفته بود is an embedded interrogative clause 'where he had gone,' with رفته بود forming the pluperfect ('had gone').
segments.s266-g23: بپردخت is a past verb meaning 'he turned (his attention)/dealt with' (pәrdaxt used poetically). زان پس 'after that' is a temporal adverbial, and به افراسیاب marks the indirect object 'to Afrasiab' with به.
segments.s266-g24: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which': با لشکر آمد 'came with an army' (آمد simple past), and به نزدیک آب 'to/near the water' places the arrival 'close to the water.' نزدیک is used substantively/adverbially after به to mean 'near.'
segments.s266-g1: نویسنده (subject) + نامه را (definite object with را) + خواند (3rd-person singular past 'read') is a normal SOV clause; و گفت ('and said') is a following verb that introduces reported speech. The past-tense verbs mark that the scribe read the letter and then spoke.
segments.s266-g2: برآورد here is the lexical verb 'bring about/produce' placed before the auxiliary خواهم (1st-person future 'I will'); this order (main verb + خواهم) is a poetic/flexible expression of the future. نهان 'the hidden' is the object and از نهفت tightens the meaning ('from concealment' / 'from what was hidden').
segments.s266-g3: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'); برگشایی is 2nd-person singular verbal form 'you open/loosen' placed before the pronoun تو (poetic inversion). لب را (the lip(s) with object marker را) ز بند = 'from the bond/restraint' (ز = poetic 'from', بند = 'fetter/restraint').
segments.s266-g4: زبان (tongue / speech) is the subject; آورد is a 3rd-person verb 'brings/causes to come' (here with future implication because of the conditional). بر سرت 'upon your head' (بر = on/upon, سرت = your head / upon you) and بر گزند places گزند ('harm') as the unfortunate result — 'harm will befall you.'
segments.s266-g5: یکی 'a certain' or 'one' introduces an indefinite actor; نامه is the object and فرمود is a past verb meaning here 'caused/sent/ordered' (classical usage can mean 'commanded' or 'sent' in context). نزدِیک شاه is the prepositional phrase indicating the recipient or the addressee (to/near the king).
segments.s266-g6: به marks purpose ('in order to'); آگاه کردن is the verbal compound 'to inform' (آگاه 'aware' + کردن auxiliary 'to make/do'); ز کار سپاه = 'concerning the business/affairs of the army' (ز = 'about/from', کار = 'affair/work', سپاه = 'army'). The whole phrase gives the purpose of sending the letter.
segments.s266-g7: به خسرو = 'to Khosrow' (recipient); نمود is past-tense 'showed/related' here; آن کجا رفته بود is a subordinate clause 'where he had gone' with رفته (past participle) + بود (auxiliary) forming the pluperfect 'had gone'. The clause as a whole: 'he showed Khosrow where he had gone.'
segments.s266-g8: سخن هرچ = 'whatever words/whatever was said'; پیران بدو = 'the elders to him' (بدو = to him); گفته بود is again the pluperfect 'had said/was said' — so this segment means he reported to Khosrow everything the elders had told him.
segments.s266-g97: همیدون is a proper name (direct object) placed before the verb, following classical Persian SOV tendencies. ببردند is a 3rd-person-plural past tense form of بردن ('they brought/took'); پیش + هجیر is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before Hojir' (پیش + proper name). Word order here is object – verb – adverbial (the adverbial 'پیش هجیر' marks whose presence the action occurred in).
segments.s266-g98: This short clause is somewhat compressed/elliptical. زین زرین is an adjective + noun ('golden saddle' with the adjective زرین following the noun as is common in older Persian poetic usage). ده اسب is a numeral + noun ('ten horses'); هژیر is the name Hojir, here marking the possessor/recipient (i.e. 'Hojir's ten horses' or 'ten horses for Hojir'). ابا is uncertain/archaic in this position (see note): it does not fit common modern verbs here and may be a scribal/orthographic fusion or an enclitic; in context the sense is best rendered pragmatically as part of the gifting phrase ('they gave/presented ... to Hojir'), but the exact lexical function of ابا in this line is unclear.
segments.s266-g99: به یارانش is a dative phrase: به + یاران + possessive enclitic ش ('to/for his companions'). بر خلعت ('بر' + خلعت) indicates 'upon (robes of honour)' — literally 'he cast/bestowed robes upon his companions.' افگند is a past tense (3rd‑person singular) 'he cast/placed/gave'; نیز 'also' adds that this action accompanies other gifts.
segments.s266-g100: درم داد is a compound of the noun درم ('dirham, coin') and the verb داد (here 3rd‑person past 'he gave'); the verb داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' rather than the abstract 'justice'. و simply coordinates items. دینار is another coin name. هرگونه چیز is a generic object phrase 'every kind of thing' (هرگونه modifying چیز). Overall this is a list of material gifts given.
segments.s266-g101: از آن پس ('after that') is a temporal phrase. چو here functions as the subordinating conjunction 'when'. از جای برخاستند is a clause meaning 'they rose from their places' — برخاستند is the 3rd‑person plural past tense 'they rose/departed'. The whole segment sets the temporal circumstance for the following action.
segments.s266-g102: نشستنگه is a compound noun meaning 'the seating/place to sit' (نشست + نگه, a locative/abstract formation). می‌بیاراستند is a 3rd‑person‑plural imperfective form (mi‑prefix + بیاراستن) used narratively to mean 'they prepared/they adorned' — in epic style this imperfective can describe a completed past action in the flow of narration.
segments.s266-g103: هجیر و بزرگان خسروپرست lists participants: هجیر (proper name) and بزرگان ('the great ones' or 'nobles'); خسروپرست is an adjectival/epithet meaning 'those devoted to/adoring Khosrow' (here characterizing the بزرگان). The coordination is simple: 'Hojir and the nobles, who are Khosrow‑worshippers.'
segments.s266-g104: گرفتند is 3rd‑person‑plural past 'they seized/took up'. یکسر is an adverb ('at once' / 'altogether') modifying the verb. همه 'all' reinforces that every one of them participated. می‌به دست is an idiomatic poetic phrase: می‌به is a classical/poetic word related to wine/drink and دست 'hand' gives the sense 'took the wine/cup in hand' — i.e. they all seized their cups and began drinking.
segments.s266-g33: به marks the indirect object (to/for); خسرو is the indirect object (proper name).رسد is a 3rd-person singular present/epistolary verb (classical usage often rendered English past/“reached”), so the phrase literally reads “to Khosrow reaches…”. زان سپس is an archaic temporal sequence (“from that, then/thereafter”) modifying آگهی, which is the noun ‘news/information’ that is reaching Khosrow.
segments.s266-g34: ک is the colloquial/poetic contraction of که introducing a content clause (“that/which”); با او is a prepositional phrase ‘with him’. چه سازد is an interrogative + 3rd-person singular verb (‘what will he/do what will he make/do’), with سازد in classical Persian functioning as the present/future 3sg. به بختت is a prepositional phrase (‘concerning/to your fate’, with enclitic ـت = your). رهی is a vocative/epitheton ‘O wayfarer’ (archaic), addressing the addressee; word order is poetic and somewhat inverted.
segments.s266-g35: و دیگر که begins a new clause (‘and another [thing], that…’), where دیگر functions as an additive/contrastive particle (‘another/moreover’). از is the preposition ‘from/of’, followed by the proper name رستم (Rostam). دیو بند is a nominal phrase: دیو ‘demon’ + بند ‘fetter/bond’ (i.e. ‘demon bound’ or ‘fetter for the demon’) — بند here is a noun meaning a fetter or restraint.
segments.s266-g36: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’; لهراسب is the proper name Lohrāsb. وز in classical poetry is often a fusion of و + ز (and + from) used to continue the series (‘and from…’), so it functions like ‘and from’. اشکش is ashk + ـش ‘his tear(s)’ (possessive suffix), and هوشمند is an adjectival predicate ‘intelligent/wise’ describing the agent affected or moved; the clause links the named persons and feelings in a compressed, poetic order.
segments.s266-g37: ز کردار ایشان is an ablative/agent phrase ‘from/of their deeds’ (ز = ‘from’, کردار = ‘deeds’, ایشان = ‘their’). به کهتر here uses کهتر (‘lesser’) adverbially or comparatively with به to indicate ‘to a lesser degree’ or ‘a scant amount’ (i.e. ‘gave little news’). خبر is the noun ‘news/report’ that is being sent.
segments.s266-g38: رساند is the simple past/perfective 3rd-person singular ‘he conveyed/sent/reported’. مگر here means ‘except’ (save), so the clause states that the report was sent to no one except the following noun. شاه پیروزگر is the object of that exception: ‘the king, the triumphant’ (a title phrase).
segments.s266-g39: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. نامه is the direct object ‘letter’. به مهر means ‘with a seal’ (مهر here = seal/stamp), and اندر آورد is a compound verb ‘brought in’ / ‘brought’ (آورد in classical usage ‘he caused/introduced/produced’). و بند links the next action: بند in poetic context commonly means a stanza or distich (i.e. he appended a poetic stanza).
segments.s266-g40: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense ‘he commanded/ordered/said’. تا introduces a purposive/result clause here (‘so that/that’). بر is the preposition ‘on/upon/toward’, ستور is ‘steed/horse’ (i.e. mounts), and نوند is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning ‘they go/they set out’ (classical third-person plural imperative/subjunctive-like form in context). The whole clause therefore gives the order to mount and depart.
segments.s266-g73: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning 'he said/ordered.' The conjunction/marker تا after a verb in classical Persian often introduces a reported imperative or result clause ('he commanded that...'); پرده برداشتند is a past plural clause 'they lifted/removed the curtain' (برداشتند = past, 3rd plural).
segments.s266-g74: به marks the direction/goal here ('onto' or 'to'), اسبش = 'his horse' (اسب + enclitic -ش 'his'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/at' used with درگاه ('threshold/royal doorway') to locate the action; بگذاشتند is past plural 'they placed' (ب‑ prefix is common in classical/poetic forms before past stems). The subject 'they' is the same attendants implied earlier.
segments.s266-g75: هجیر اندر آمد is a straightforward past clause 'Hejir came in' (اندر = locative 'in/into' placed before the verb, as is common in older Persian). چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'; خسرو is the addressee/figure looked at (here the royal person). The form بدوی in this line is dialectal/poetic and does not match the ordinary senses listed — here it functions as an archaic/clitic-like element (= بدو + ی 'to him' / 'toward him') and should be read as pointing the action toward Khosrow.
segments.s266-g76: نگه کرد is a light‑verb construction: نگه (a 'look' or 'gaze') + کرد 'made/did' = 'fixed/turned a look' (past). پیشش 'before him/in his presence' marks the direction/target of the following action. بمالید (past) means 'he rubbed/pressed' and روی is the object 'face' — together 'he rubbed/pressed his face before him.' The subject is implied from context (the newcomer performing a respectful gesture).
segments.s266-g77: بپرسید is past 'he asked' (ب‑ prefix appears in classical usage). بسیار here quantifies the asking — 'asked much/many [questions]'. و links the next action; بنشاندش is past causative/caused form 'he made him sit' / 'seated him' (نشاند + enclitic -ش = 'him').
segments.s266-g78: Word order is emphatic: 'هزاران (thousands) هجیر (Hejir) آفرین خواندش' — literally 'thousands, Hejir, afarin he-called-him.' آفرین خواندش is an idiom 'he cried “Afarin” to him' meaning 'he praised him repeatedly'; خواندش here is past 'he uttered/called to him' (the object clitic -ش = 'him').
segments.s266-g79: ز + گوهر is a genitive/partitive 'from the gems/of the jewels'; یکی 'one (of)' selects a single item; تاج پیروزه شاه = 'the king's turquoise crown' (پیروزه is the adjective 'turquoise' describing تاج, and شاه names the owner). The phrase lists the object being given.
segments.s266-g80: به سر 'onto the head' (به = onto/toward, سر = head) with بر before the verb expressing location ('upon'). نهادش is the past of نهاد used here as a plain past 'he placed/put' with the object clitic -ش. چو introduces a simile 'like/as'; رخشنده ماه = 'the shining/radiant moon' — the crown is compared to the bright moon.
segments.s266-g9: فرستادن is a verbal noun (masdar) here meaning 'the sending' or 'sending of'; it precedes the list of people being sent. گیو, پیوند and مهر are three coordinated items joined by و; in this poetic context پیوند and مهر function as proper names rather than their common nouns. Word order is masdar + objects (names).
segments.s266-g10: نمودن here is used in a causative/performative sense 'to make/show/appoint' following the preceding noun phrase (so: 'he made/appointed ...'). بدو = 'to him' (dative). کار گردان سپهر is a poetic nominal phrase: کارگردان (literally 'turner/manager of work') + سپهر ('the sky/heavens'), i.e. 'the one who directs the heavens' (a poetic agent). The phrase as a whole means that the heavens (or their director) entrusted or assigned the task to him.
segments.s266-g11: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' (introducing the source). پاسَخ + که + دادند is a relative clause: 'the answer that they gave'. دادند is past tense 'they gave'. مر is an older/dialectal dative pronoun 'to me'. گیو را is the (definite) object phrase marked with را and is what the answer concerned: 'about Giv' / 'concerning Giv' in this construction.
segments.s266-g12: بزرگان و فرزانه is a coordinated noun phrase 'the great ones and the wise' (the elders and sages) that functions as the agents in the previous clause (they = دادند). نیو را is an additional definite noun phrase marked by را; in context this is a proper name, not the common noun senses listed, so it should be read as a name. The word order places the agents before this object phrase.
segments.s266-g13: وز here is a poetic contraction (roughly 'و ز' = 'and from'/'and of'), introducing the following group. آن لشکری = 'that army'. کز is the relative particle 'that/which'. پسش = 'after him' / 'following him' (پس + -ش enclitic). چون پلنگ = a simile 'like a leopard' modifying the action of the army.
segments.s266-g14: بیاورد is a narrative verb form ('brought' / 'rushed' / 'came'); in classical Persian forms like بیاورد often appear where modern Persian would use simple past. سوی is the preposition 'toward'. کنابد here describes exertion/straining into action (a poetic verb meaning to strive/press on), and به جنگ = 'into/to the battle'. Together: the army came/rushed toward and pressed into the fight.
segments.s266-g15: از آن پس = 'after that' (temporal). کجا here functions in a distributive/indefinite sense 'wherever' (typical in poetry). رزمگه is a noun 'battlefield' (rendezvous/place of battle). ساختند is past tense 'they built/made' — so 'wherever they made a battlefield'.
segments.s266-g16: وز again is the poetic connective (≈ 'and from' / 'and of'). آن رزم = 'that battle/combat'. دل را uses را to mark the definite direct object 'the heart' (i.e. their hearts). بپرداختند is a compound verb meaning 'they paid/turned/ devoted' — here 'they devoted (their) heart(s) to it' or 'they set their hearts upon that fight'.
segments.s266-g25: گر is the conditional 'if'. او is the third-person pronoun 'he'. از لب رود جیحون is a prepositional phrase placed before the verb meaning 'from the bank/edge of the Jihun (Amu Darya)' — لب = 'bank/brink' and رود = 'river'. سپاه is the noun 'army' introduced here as the thing being moved (its explicit object marker appears later).
segments.s266-g26: گذارد is the verb 'to place/send' governed by the conditional 'گر'; به ایران is the directional complement 'into/toward Iran'. سپه را is the same noun 'army' repeated with the object marker را (makes it definite/object of گذارد), and به راه ('on the road') is a locative/complement showing that he 'sets the army on the march.' Poetic word order allows the object and complements to appear around the verb.
segments.s266-g27: تو دانی is 'you know' (present). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'. با او نداریم پای literally 'we do not have a foot/footing with him' — پای here means 'foot/standing, a base' so the clause means 'we have no standing/chance against him.'
segments.s266-g28: ایا is a rhetorical/exclamatory particle here ('is it not / indeed?'). فرخجسته جهان is a compound epithet 'fortunate/illustrious of the world' (adjective + world). کدخدای is a title literally 'kād-khudā' (headman/chief); together this asks rhetorically whether he is not the fortunate chief/lord of the world.
segments.s266-g29: مگر here functions as 'unless' (introducing the exception). خسرو is the king's name/title and آید is 'comes'. به پشت سپاه means 'to the rear/behind the army' (پشت = 'rear/behind').
segments.s266-g30: به سر is the locative 'on the head'. بر نهد is the verb 'to place / lay upon' (نهاد with the prefix بر- 'upon'). بندگان را uses را to mark the definite/affected people ('the subjects/people' — بندگان here = subjects/people), and کلاه is the thing placed ('hat'). The clause literally says 'he places hats on the heads of the people.'
segments.s266-g31: ور is a conditional 'if'. ایدونک is a particle (poetic/colloquial) meaning roughly 'now / in that case'. پیران کند دست پیش is an idiomatic sequence: پیران 'the elders', کند 'do/make', and دست پیش 'to put the hand forward' meaning 'to take the initiative / make the first move / extend the hand.'
segments.s266-g32: بخواهد is 'he will want/ask for' (optative/future sense). سپه یاور is a compound meaning 'military aid / an ally/helper for the army' (سپه = army, یاور = helper/ally). از شاه خویش means 'from his own king' (خویش formed from خوی + ش = 'his own'). So the clause says he will request military support from his king.
segments.s266-g49: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). مر is an archaic dative pronoun 'to me' (placed before the rest of the phrase); تو را is 'you' with the object/indirect-object marker را. نزد من دستگاه is a prepositional phrase 'a post/seat in my presence' (دستگاه here = court/establishment). The segment sets up the conditional 'if you seek a place at my court (with me)'.
segments.s266-g50: همی is the archaic continuous/prefixal marker that makes the verb ongoing; جست here is the root 'seek' (with همی giving a present/habitual sense). باید is a modal 'must/ought', and کنونست گاه is a compound assertion 'now is the time' (کنونست = 'it is now', گاه = 'time/occasion'). Together the clause says that now is the proper time to seek the post.
segments.s266-g51: چو here = 'when' (introducing a temporal clause). بستانی is the subjunctive/imperative-like form of 'take/receive' ('when you take/receive'). این نامه 'this letter' is the direct object, and هم در زمان adds 'also/at the appointed time' (در زمان = 'at the time' / 'in due time').
segments.s266-g52: برو is the simple imperative 'go'. هم ('also/at once') reinforces immediacy. به کردار = 'in/according to the manner/behavior', where کردار is 'deed/conduct'. باد functions as an optative particle 'may it be' (classical wish construction). دمان (دِمان) here is a noun 'skirt/hem' (literal or metaphorical); the whole clause is a formulaic injunction: go and let your conduct be like the wind at the hem (i.e., swift, light).
segments.s266-g53: شب و روز is an adverbial phrase 'night and day' (i.e., continuously). ماسای and سر are proper names (agents/dispatchers) coordinated by و. بر + مخار here is a short directional/locative phrase; word order places the time expression and the named messengers before the verb in the next segment, so these words function as adverbial and subject/agent.
segments.s266-g54: ببر is the imperative of بردن 'to carry/take' (here 'take/carry!'). نامه من is 'my letter' (من after the noun marks the possessor), and بر شهریار is the directional phrase 'to the king' (بر = toward/to, شهریار = king). The whole is a direct command to carry the letter to the king.
segments.s266-g55: This clause uses the light-verb construction پدرود کردن 'to take leave/offer a farewell'. گرفتش is the past tense 'he seized/took him' with the enclitic object -ش 'him/it' (so 'he took him [to say farewell]'). The repeated preposition به introduces the act/context; the sequence describes that he received/held him in a farewell gesture.
segments.s266-g56: برون آمد is the compound past 'came out/went forth'. از پیش is the prepositional phrase 'from before/in the presence of', so از + پیش = 'from before'. فرخ پدر is a descriptive nominal group 'the fortunate father' (فرخ = fortunate/happy, پدر = father). Together: 'he went out from before his fortunate father.'
segments.s266-g105: نشستند is a third-person plural past tense (classical past) meaning 'they sat/assembled' (the provided lemma list does not include this sense). یک روز و یک شب is a temporal expression 'one day and one night' (duration). به هم = 'together/with one another' and attaches to the verb phrase to give 'they sat together for a day and a night.' Word order places the time expression after the verb, which is normal in poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s266-g106: همی here is a continuative/adverbial particle 'kept/continually'. رای زد is an idiom meaning 'gave counsel / deliberated' (رای = counsel/plan; زد is the verb used idiomatically 'to strike/express' — here 'to express/offer' an opinion). خسرو is the subject (Khosrow). از بیش و کم = 'from more and less' = 'the pros and cons / in all respects', with از marking the domain concerning which he deliberated.
segments.s266-g107: به شبگیر is a temporal/temporalizing phrase 'at nightfall / as night came'. خسرو is the subject. سر و تن (head and body) are the direct objects placed before the verb (typical Persian word order). بشست is the simple past third-person singular 'he washed' (classical form with the prefix ب-).
segments.s266-g108: به پیش = 'into the presence of / before'. جهانداور is an epithet meaning 'World‑judge' (here a title for the divine judge). آمد is the past tense 'came' (third-person singular). نخست is an adverb 'first/first of all' modifying the action, placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s266-g109: بپوشید is past third-person singular 'he put on / he clothed himself' (classical form with ب-). نو جامه بندگی is a noun phrase 'the new garment of servitude/devotion' (adjective نو precedes the noun جامه; بندگی is a genitive/attributive noun 'servitude/devotion'). The verb governs the whole object phrase.
segments.s266-g110: دو دیده = 'two eyes' (his two eyes). چو = simile particle 'like/as'. ابری here functions as a noun 'a cloud' (used figuratively). به بارندگی literally 'with/on rain' -> 'ready to rain' / 'full of tears' — the preposition به + بارندگی gives a circumstantial phrase describing the cloud-like, rain‑laden state of the eyes.
segments.s266-g111: دوتایی شده is a participial phrase meaning 'having become two / the two together' or more simply 'the two of them (together)'. پشت و بنهاد سر is a compound action: پشت = 'back', و = 'and', بنهاد (here a past/preterite of بنهادن) = 'placed/put down' → 'put (their) heads down' or 'bent their heads'; word order places the objects before/around the verb in this poetic line. Overall it describes a joint act of bowing/prostrating.
segments.s266-g112: همی is the continuative particle 'continually / kept on'. آفرین is used as an acclamation 'praise/Bravo' (object of خواند). خواند is past third-person singular 'he/they read/uttered/recited'. بر here means 'toward / to' (introducing the beneficiary/recipient), and دادگر 'the Just One' is the addressee (a title for the righteous judge/God). The clause means they repeatedly cried out a praise to the Just One.

sentences.s266-s21: They built a royal audience-hall, and many horsemen rode forth.
sentences.s266-s22: He ordered that Hijir go before him — a young man who seemed both shrewd and aged.
sentences.s266-s23: The hero spoke that whole speech to the clear‑sighted young man.
sentences.s266-s24: He said to him, 'O son of the vigilant-hearted, dispatch one swift detachment for this task.'
sentences.s266-s29: He summoned two men from the host to himself and set their light-armed ones upon swift steeds.
sentences.s266-s30: He went out from his father's curtained chamber; at every stopping-place he mounted another beast.
sentences.s266-s31: Their food, sleep and rest were upon their steeds—whether in the darkness of night or in the shining light.
sentences.s266-s32: Thus moving in that manner they set out on the road, and within about a week they came near the king.
sentences.s266-s33: When a horseman came from the road of Iran, a man arrived before the renowned Khosrow.
sentences.s266-s34: He sent people to receive Shamak, asking, 'What sort of bold, insolent men are these?'
sentences.s266-s35: They asked, when they saw Hajir's face, 'Who are you, O champion-born, lion-catcher?'
sentences.s266-s36: They greeted him, saying, 'Well met — you have come so suddenly into the presence of the king of the world.'
sentences.s266-s41: The king questioned Goudarz and the chiefs of the army, asking each one individually.
sentences.s266-s42: He gave the nobles' salutations to Khosrow, and reminded him of all the army's affairs.
sentences.s266-s43: Then he gave him the champion's letter — the young, wise, bright‑souled one.
sentences.s266-s44: They seated the scribe before him; he commanded that the letter be read aloud.
sentences.s266-s45: When he read the letter to Khosrow the scribe, Hojir's mouth shone like ruby.
sentences.s266-s46: Then (they) went and, after that, said to the treasury: 'Bring dinars and brocade from the hidden store.'
sentences.s266-s47: She (or it) brought out the moon‑like (beauty) when she heard the command; it kept falling until his head became unseen.
sentences.s266-s48: Then he brought a gold‑embroidered robe, such as (one sees) from the king's gate.
sentences.s266-s57: He asked him for victory and nobility, and sought from him villagers and a moon‑bright throne.
sentences.s266-s58: He lamented to God about Afrasiab; in his anguish water poured from his two eyes.
sentences.s266-s59: From there he came like a dark cypress and sat at the imperial court.
sentences.s266-s9: He remembered in his heart the warrior Homan and Nastihehn completely.
sentences.s266-s10: Concerning Bijan's deed on the day of battle, what did Turan's mace‑bearers do to him?
sentences.s266-s11: He had told the whole tale from end to end, as everyone had said, where he had gone from that fight and war.
sentences.s266-s12: After that he turned to Afrasiab, who with an army came near the water.
sentences.s266-s1: The scribe read the letter and said, "I will bring to light what is concealed."
sentences.s266-s2: If you loosen your lips from their restraint, speech will spring forth and bring harm upon your head.
sentences.s266-s3: He sent a letter to the king to inform him about the army's movements.
sentences.s266-s4: He showed Khosrow where he had gone and (related) whatever the elders had told him.
sentences.s266-s49: They brought Hamidun before Hojir; to Hojir they gave a golden saddle and ten horses.
sentences.s266-s50: He cast robes upon his companions as well; he gave dirhams and dinars and every kind of thing.
sentences.s266-s51: After that, when they rose from their places, they prepared the seating.
sentences.s266-s52: Hojir and the great Khosrow‑worshippers took up (their) cups of wine all at once.
sentences.s266-s17: After that, news of it reached Khosrow, (asking) what he would do with him — “O wayfarer, what of your fate?”
sentences.s266-s18: And another thing: (that) from Rostam the demon was bound; from Lohrasp and from his tears the wise one (was moved).
sentences.s266-s19: He sent scant news of their deeds — informing none but the triumphant king.
sentences.s266-s20: When he brought the letter in sealed and composed a stanza, he ordered that they mount their steeds and go forth.
sentences.s266-s37: He ordered that they lift the curtain, and they set him on his horse at the gateway.
sentences.s266-s38: Hejir entered; when Khosrow looked (at him), he fixed his gaze and rubbed his face before him.
sentences.s266-s39: He asked many questions and had him seated; he cried 'Afarin!' to Hejir a thousand times.
sentences.s266-s40: From the jewels he took one turquoise crown of the king and placed it upon his head like the radiant moon.
sentences.s266-s5: He sent Giv, Peyvand and Mehr; the director of the heavens gave him the charge.
sentences.s266-s6: From the answers the elders and the wise gave me about Giv and Niu.
sentences.s266-s7: And of that army which followed him, like a leopard, rushed toward the fight.
sentences.s266-s8: After that, wherever they made a battlefield, they devoted their hearts to that combat.
sentences.s266-s13: If he sends an army from the banks of the Jihun into Iran and sets that army on the march.
sentences.s266-s14: You know that we have no footing against him; is he not the fortunate lord of the world?
sentences.s266-s15: Unless Khosrow comes to the rear of the army and places hats on the heads of the people.
sentences.s266-s16: If now the elders take the initiative (extend their hand first), he will ask his own king for an army's aid.
sentences.s266-s25: If you seek a place at my court with me, now is the time.
sentences.s266-s26: When you receive this letter, go at once, and let your conduct be like the wind at the hem.
sentences.s266-s27: Night and day, Masāy and Sar, carry my letter to the king.
sentences.s266-s28: He clasped him in farewell and then went out from before his fortunate father.
sentences.s266-s53: They sat together for a day and a night; Khosrow kept weighing the pros and cons.
sentences.s266-s54: At nightfall Khosrow washed his head and body; then he first came before the World-judge.
sentences.s266-s55: He put on the new garment of servitude; his two eyes, like clouds, were full of rain.
sentences.s266-s56: The two turned their backs and bowed their heads; they kept chanting 'Afarin' to the Just One.
